items.reality: It's O(n) ONLY when each index enters and leaves the window O(1) times. If you re-scan the window per step, you're back to O(n·k) or O(n²).
items.example:
```python
# BAD: recomputes sum each step → O(n·k)
for l in range(n - k + 1):
    if sum(arr[l:l+k]) >= K: ...
# GOOD: rolling sum → O(n)
run = sum(arr[:k])
for r in range(k, n):
    run += arr[r] - arr[r - k]
```

items.reality: Two-pointer is a *family* — opposite ends, fast/slow, and dual-sequence merges all qualify. The unifying idea is 'one O(n) loop, two cursors with disciplined movement'.
items.example:
```python
# Opposite ends: l = 0, r = n - 1
# Fast/slow:    slow = 0, fast = 1..n-1
# Dual-seq:    i over A, j over B (merge)
```

items.myth: Sliding window can find any 'best subarray of length k' problem.
items.reality: Only when the metric is incrementally updatable in O(1). Sum and count are. Max/min require a monotonic deque (still O(n) amortized) — median requires balanced BSTs / heaps.
items.example:
```python
# Rolling sum: O(1) per step.
# Rolling max: needs a monotonic deque.
# Rolling median: two heaps (or SortedList).
```

items.myth: Two-pointer always beats the hash-table approach.
items.reality: Two-pointer needs sorted data. If you can't (or shouldn't) sort, a hash-table O(n) is usually equal or better; you trade O(n) memory for not having to sort.
items.example:
```python
# Sorted + two-pointer: O(n log n) (sort) + O(n).
# Unsorted + hash:     O(n) time, O(n) memory.
```
